remedies.name: Chamomile
preps: Dried flower tea — steep 1–2 tsp in hot water 5–10 min
Evening tea with honey and lemon
Cold compress infusion for headache comfort
remedies.summary: One of the most widely used household herbs in European tradition. Historically prepared as tea for calming nerves, settling the stomach, and easing mild headaches. Used in ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome for thousands of years.
remedies.modern: Contains the flavonoid apigenin, studied for its interaction with GABA receptors. Also contains bisabolol and chamazulene. Research ongoing and preliminary.
remedies.safety: May cause allergic reactions in those sensitive to Asteraceae family (ragweed, chrysanthemums). May interact with blood-thinning medications. Consult provider if pregnant.
remedies.serving: 1–3 cups of tea daily is traditional. Historical guidance only.

remedies.name: Turmeric
preps: Golden milk — simmered with milk, black pepper, and fat
Turmeric paste in warm water or smoothies
remedies.summary: Used in Ayurveda for over 4,000 years. Known as Jiang Huang in TCM. Traditionally supports digestion, joint comfort, and healthy inflammatory response.
remedies.modern: Curcumin is the primary bioactive. Bioavailability enhanced by piperine (black pepper). Extensively studied for antioxidant properties.
remedies.safety: Safe as culinary spice. High-dose supplements may cause digestive upset. May interact with blood-thinners and diabetes drugs. Caution with gallbladder disease.
remedies.serving: ¼–½ tsp ground turmeric in food or golden milk. Best with black pepper and fat.

remedies.name: Ashwagandha
preps: Root powder in warm milk with honey
Blended into warm beverages or smoothies
remedies.summary: Classified as Rasayana and Medhya Rasayana in Ayurveda. Used 3,000+ years for stress resilience, vitality, and calming the mind. Known as 'Indian ginseng' though unrelated to true ginseng.
remedies.modern: Withanolides are primary bioactive compounds. Some clinical trials explore stress markers; research ongoing and not conclusive.
remedies.safety: May cause digestive upset. Consult provider with thyroid conditions. Not recommended in pregnancy. May interact with sedatives, thyroid meds, immunosuppressants.
remedies.serving: Traditionally ¼–½ tsp root powder in warm milk. Historical context only.

remedies.name: Ginger
preps: Ginger-honey-lemon for throat and cold comfort
Candied or dried as digestive aid
remedies.summary: One of the most widely used roots in history. Sheng Jiang (fresh) / Gan Jiang (dried) in TCM. Warming herb supporting digestion, circulation, and comfort during seasonal challenges.
remedies.modern: Gingerols and shogaols are primary bioactives. Some research supports use for nausea in specific contexts.
remedies.safety: Generally safe. May cause heartburn in sensitive individuals. High amounts may interact with blood-thinners. Consult provider with gallstones.
remedies.serving: A few thin slices in hot water for tea, or freely in cooking.

remedies.name: Holy Basil (Tulsi)
preps: Fresh or dried leaf tea — steep 5–10 min
Combined with ginger and honey for respiratory comfort
remedies.summary: 'Queen of Herbs' in Ayurveda. Classified as Rasayana. Sacred plant in Hindu culture for thousands of years.
remedies.modern: Eugenol and rosmarinic acid studied for antioxidant properties. Preliminary research on adaptogenic effects.
remedies.safety: Well-tolerated as tea. Mild blood-thinning effects possible. Not in high amounts during pregnancy. May affect blood sugar.
remedies.serving: 1–2 cups of tea daily is traditional.

remedies.name: Reishi Mushroom
preps: Dried slices simmered 30+ min as decoction
Powdered in broth or hot water
remedies.summary: Ling Zhi ('Spirit Mushroom') in TCM. Listed in Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing as superior herb. 'Mushroom of Immortality.'
remedies.modern: Beta-glucans studied for immune modulation. Triterpenes show bioactive properties in lab. Clinical evidence developing.
remedies.safety: May cause digestive upset or dryness. May interact with blood-thinners and immunosuppressants.
remedies.serving: Decoction or small amounts of powder.

remedies.name: Valerian Root
preps: Dried root tea — steep 10–15 min (strong earthy flavor)
Combined with lemon balm or hops
remedies.summary: Recommended by Galen for insomnia. Popular in medieval Europe. Name may derive from Latin 'valere' (to be strong).
remedies.modern: Valerenic acid studied for GABA receptor interaction. Mixed but generally favorable findings for mild sleep difficulties.
remedies.safety: May cause headache, dizziness, digestive upset. Not with sedatives, alcohol, or CNS depressants. Not in pregnancy.
remedies.serving: Tea 30–60 min before bedtime.

remedies.name: Lemon Balm
preps: Fresh or dried leaf tea — 5–10 min
remedies.summary: Paracelsus called it 'elixir of life.' Avicenna recommended for mood. Carmelite nuns created Carmelite Water in the 14th century.
remedies.modern: Rosmarinic acid studied as antioxidant. Preliminary mood and cognition studies.
remedies.safety: Very well-tolerated. Thyroid conditions: consult provider at high doses. May enhance sedatives.
remedies.serving: 1–3 cups daily, often afternoon or evening.

remedies.name: Peppermint
preps: Fresh or dried leaf tea — 5–7 min
Fresh leaves in water or dishes
Aromatic use for clarity and congestion
remedies.summary: Used centuries in European and Middle Eastern traditions. Leaves found in Egyptian pyramids (~1000 BCE). Used for digestion and headache relief.
remedies.modern: Menthol is primary active. Peppermint oil studied for digestive comfort with some positive findings.
remedies.safety: May worsen GERD. Oil not for young children. Consult provider with bile duct issues.
remedies.serving: 1–3 cups tea daily.

remedies.name: Lavender
remedies.summary: Used since ancient Rome for bathing and relaxation. From Latin 'lavare' (to wash). Widely used for calming and headache comfort.
remedies.modern: Linalool studied for nervous system interaction. Aromatherapy research preliminary.
remedies.safety: Well-tolerated as tea/culinary. Oil not for ingestion. Some topical sensitivity.
remedies.serving: 1 cup tea in the evening.

remedies.name: Passionflower
remedies.summary: Used by indigenous peoples of the Americas before European contact. Documented by Spanish explorers. Adopted into European pharmacopoeia.
remedies.modern: Chrysin and flavonoids studied for GABA interaction. Preliminary positive findings for anxiety and sleep.
remedies.safety: May enhance sedatives. Not in pregnancy. May interact with blood-thinners.
remedies.serving: 1 cup tea in the evening.

remedies.name: Elderberry
preps: Cooked into syrup (MUST cook — raw not safe)
remedies.summary: Hippocrates called the elder tree his 'medicine chest.' Widely used in European folk medicine and by Indigenous North American peoples.
remedies.modern: Rich in anthocyanins. Some clinical trials on seasonal immune support with positive findings.
remedies.safety: NEVER consume raw (cyanogenic glycosides). Always cook. May interact with immunosuppressants, diabetes drugs. Autoimmune: consult provider.
remedies.serving: 1–2 tsp elderberry syrup during seasonal challenges. Always cooked.

remedies.name: Lion's Mane Mushroom
preps: Dried powder in tea or coffee
remedies.summary: Hou Tou Gu in TCM. Associated with supporting five organs, vitality, and cognitive health. Prized as culinary and medicinal mushroom.
remedies.modern: Hericenones and erinacines studied for potential NGF stimulation. Human research limited.
remedies.safety: Well-tolerated as food. Avoid with mushroom allergies. May interact with blood-thinners.
remedies.serving: Consumed as food or dried powder.

remedies.name: Garlic
remedies.summary: Given to Egyptian pyramid builders. Used in TCM, Ayurveda, Greek medicine. Appears in Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE). Traditional cold and sore throat remedy across cultures.
remedies.modern: Allicin and organosulfur compounds extensively studied for antimicrobial and antioxidant properties.
remedies.safety: Very safe as food. Raw may cause digestive discomfort. May interact with blood-thinners. Inform surgeon before surgery.
remedies.serving: Freely as culinary food. Some traditions use 1–2 raw cloves daily.

remedies.name: Stinging Nettle
remedies.summary: European spring tonic for centuries. First wild green of spring. Native American peoples also used nettle. One of the most mineral-dense plants.
remedies.modern: Rich in iron, calcium, magnesium, silica. Some research on seasonal allergies; evidence limited.
remedies.safety: Cook/dry before use (fresh stings). May interact with blood-thinners, diabetes drugs, BP meds. Diuretic effects.
remedies.serving: Cooked freely as food. Tea as long infusion.

remedies.name: Dandelion
preps: Young leaves in salads or sautéed
remedies.summary: Pu Gong Ying in TCM. Spring tonic in European folk medicine. Consumed as nutritious wild green. Root supports digestion; leaves are mineral-rich.
remedies.modern: Root contains inulin (prebiotic). Bitter compounds may stimulate digestive secretions.
remedies.safety: Well-tolerated. Asteraceae allergy risk. May interact with diuretics, lithium, diabetes meds. Avoid pesticide areas.
remedies.serving: Leaves as food; root tea 1–2 cups daily.

remedies.name: Bone Broth
preps: Strain; consume warm or as soup base
remedies.summary: Found in every traditional cuisine. TCM considers it deeply nourishing. European folk use chicken broth during illness — 'Jewish penicillin.'
remedies.modern: Contains gelatin, glycine, minerals (amounts vary). Some research on glycine for gut lining.
remedies.safety: Very well-tolerated. Histamine-sensitive may react. Quality bones recommended. Some lead content concerns.
remedies.serving: 1–2 cups warm broth daily is traditional.

remedies.name: Sauerkraut
remedies.summary: Among the oldest preserved foods. Staples in European (sauerkraut), Korean (kimchi), Japanese (tsukemono) traditions. Captain Cook carried it to prevent scurvy.
remedies.modern: Fermented foods and gut microbiome diversity receiving significant scientific interest. Lactobacillus studied.
remedies.safety: Newcomers may have temporary bloating. Histamine sensitivity possible. Can be high in sodium.
remedies.serving: A few tablespoons alongside meals.

remedies.name: Miso
preps: Dissolved in warm (not boiling) water for soup
remedies.summary: Daily staple in Japan for 1,300+ years. Foundational health food. Samurai consumed daily. White, red, barley varieties reflect regional traditions.
remedies.modern: Contains live cultures. Fermentation increases nutrient bioavailability. Isoflavone content studied.
remedies.safety: High sodium. Contains soy (allergy risk). Unpasteurized has live cultures.
remedies.serving: Small bowl of soup daily (traditional Japanese).

remedies.name: Oats (Oat Straw)
remedies.summary: Staple in Scotland, Ireland, Northern Europe. Oat straw valued in Western herbalism as nervine tonic for stress and depletion.
remedies.modern: Beta-glucan well-studied for cholesterol/blood sugar. Avenanthramides are unique antioxidants.
remedies.safety: Very well-tolerated. Celiac: use certified gluten-free. Small number react to avenin.
remedies.serving: Daily as porridge. Oat straw as long infusion.

remedies.name: Flaxseed
remedies.summary: Cultivated in Babylon by 3000 BCE. Charlemagne required consumption. Hippocrates recommended for digestion.
remedies.modern: ALA is essential omega-3. Lignans studied for phytoestrogenic properties.
remedies.safety: Take with adequate water. May interact with blood-thinners. Hormone-sensitive: consult provider.
remedies.serving: 1–2 tbsp ground seed daily.

remedies.name: Sweet Potato
remedies.summary: Staple in Okinawan diet (noted longevity culture). Cultivated thousands of years in Americas. Central to African and Pacific Island traditions.
remedies.modern: Rich in beta-carotene. Complex carbs for sustained energy. Purple varieties contain anthocyanins.
remedies.safety: Very well-tolerated. Monitor potassium if needed. Oxalates may concern kidney stone–prone.
remedies.serving: Consumed as staple food.

remedies.name: Dark Leafy Greens
preps: Sautéed with garlic and olive oil
remedies.summary: Central to every food culture. Collard greens in Southern US/West African traditions. Bok choy in Asian cuisines. Most nutrient-dense foods available.
remedies.modern: Meaningful folate, magnesium, iron, vitamin K. Magnesium in hundreds of enzymatic processes.
remedies.safety: Warfarin users: keep vitamin K consistent. Raw spinach/chard oxalates reduced by cooking.
remedies.serving: Consumed daily in traditional diets.

remedies.name: Seaweed
remedies.summary: Dietary staple in Japan/Korea for thousands of years. Foundational health food. Irish, Scottish, Polynesian traditions also.
remedies.modern: Richest natural iodine source. Fucoidan studied for bioactive properties.
remedies.safety: Iodine can be very high (esp. kelp/kombu). Excess iodine may affect thyroid. Heavy metals depend on source. Thyroid conditions: consult provider.
remedies.serving: Small regular amounts (traditional Japanese approach).

remedies.name: Cinnamon (Ceylon)
remedies.summary: Among the most prized ancient spices. Mentioned in Chinese texts, the Bible, Egyptian records. Warming spice for digestion and circulation.
remedies.modern: Cinnamaldehyde studied for antimicrobial/antioxidant properties. Blood sugar research mixed. Ceylon has far less coumarin than cassia.
remedies.safety: Ceylon safe in culinary amounts. Cassia has hepatotoxic coumarin risk. May interact with diabetes meds.
remedies.serving: ¼–½ tsp in food. Ceylon preferred for daily use.

remedies.name: Tart Cherry
preps: Dried in porridge or trail mix
remedies.summary: Cultivated in European orchards for centuries. Valued seasonal fruit preserved as jams, juices, dried preparations.
remedies.modern: One of few natural melatonin food sources. Anthocyanins studied for anti-inflammatory properties.
remedies.safety: Very safe. Juice high in sugars. May interact with blood-thinners.
remedies.serving: As fruit or juice.

remedies.name: Walnuts
remedies.summary: Brain support in Persian/Middle Eastern medicine (Doctrine of Signatures). Cultivated thousands of years. Mediterranean dietary staple.
remedies.modern: Richest plant ALA omega-3 source. Polyphenols studied for antioxidant properties.
remedies.safety: Tree nut allergy common and severe. High calories. Very safe without allergies.
remedies.serving: A handful (~1 oz) daily.

remedies.name: Slippery Elm
remedies.summary: Native American peoples used inner bark as food and medicine. Adopted in American herbal practice. Mucilaginous properties soothe tissues and throats.
remedies.modern: Mucilage forms soothing film. Demulcent properties well-documented.
remedies.safety: May slow medication absorption — take 2 hours apart. Sustainability concerns with wild harvest.
remedies.serving: Bark powder in water. Lozenges as needed.

remedies.name: Fennel
preps: Seeds steeped as tea — 1 tsp crushed
remedies.summary: Xiao Hui Xiang in TCM. One of the most valued Ayurvedic digestive herbs. Chewed after meals across India.
remedies.modern: Anethole is primary aromatic. Studied for carminative properties. Traditional use well-documented.
remedies.safety: Safe in culinary amounts. Not in medicinal amounts during pregnancy. Hormone-sensitive: consult provider.
remedies.serving: Seeds as tea or chewed after meals.

remedies.name: Astragalus
preps: Dried root in soups and broths
remedies.summary: Huang Qi in TCM. Qi tonic — among most important herbs for strengthening defensive energy (Wei Qi). Used 2,000+ years.
remedies.modern: Astragalosides and polysaccharides studied for immune modulation. Clinical evidence developing.
remedies.safety: May interact with immunosuppressants. Not during acute infections (TCM practice).
remedies.serving: Root slices in soup or decoction.

remedies.name: Licorice Root
remedies.summary: Gan Cao ('sweet herb') in TCM — most frequently used herb in Chinese formulas. Used in Egyptian, Greek, Ayurvedic traditions. Root is 50× sweeter than sugar. Premier sore throat remedy.
remedies.modern: Glycyrrhizin is primary compound. DGL studied for digestive comfort.
remedies.safety: IMPORTANT: Glycyrrhizin can cause potassium depletion, water retention, elevated BP in excess. DGL safer. Contraindicated with hypertension, kidney/heart disease. Many drug interactions.
remedies.serving: Small amounts in tea. DGL preferred for regular use.

remedies.name: Hawthorn Berry
preps: Combined with hibiscus or rose hips
remedies.summary: One of oldest European medicinal plants. Associated with heart health since 1st century. Shan Zha in TCM. Premier European cardiovascular herb.
remedies.modern: OPCs and flavonoids studied for cardiovascular properties. Mixed clinical results.
remedies.safety: Heart conditions or cardiac meds: CONSULT PROVIDER. May interact with BP meds, digoxin, blood-thinners.
remedies.serving: Berry tea or syrup.

remedies.name: Marshmallow Root
preps: Cold infusion — root in room-temp water overnight
remedies.summary: Original 'marshmallow' — Egyptians combined root mucilage with honey. Centuries of European and Middle Eastern use for soothing tissues, sore throats, coughs.
remedies.modern: Rich in mucilage polysaccharides. Demulcent properties well-documented.
remedies.safety: Very well-tolerated. May slow medication absorption — take 2 hours apart.
remedies.serving: Cold infusion or warm tea.

remedies.name: Schisandra Berry
remedies.summary: Wu Wei Zi ('Five Flavor Berry') — all five flavors. Superior tonic in Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing. Russian Nanai hunters used for endurance.
remedies.modern: Lignans (schisandrin, gomisin) studied for hepatoprotective and adaptogenic properties. Western evidence limited.
remedies.safety: May interact with liver-metabolized meds (CYP450). Not in pregnancy. May cause heartburn.
remedies.serving: Dried berries in tea or decoction.

remedies.name: Hibiscus
remedies.summary: Consumed across West Africa, Mexico, Egypt. Valued as cooling, refreshing tonic.
remedies.modern: Anthocyanins and organic acids studied. Some clinical trials on BP support with positive findings.
remedies.safety: May lower BP — consult provider if on meds. Not in medicinal amounts during pregnancy.
remedies.serving: 1–3 cups tea daily.

remedies.name: Raw Honey
preps: In warm (not boiling) water or tea
remedies.summary: One of oldest medicines known. Used in virtually every tradition. Egyptian papyri, Ayurveda, Greek medicine document use. Prized for soothing throats and coughs.
remedies.modern: Some research supports honey for cough in children over 1. Antimicrobial properties documented. Manuka honey specifically studied.
remedies.safety: NEVER for children under 1 year (botulism risk). High in sugars. Not a substitute for medical treatment.
remedies.serving: 1–2 tsp in warm water or tea.

remedies.name: Rhodiola
remedies.summary: Used by Vikings and Russian cosmonauts. 'Golden root' / 'Arctic root.' Scandinavian and Siberian folk medicine for endurance and mental resilience.
remedies.modern: Rosavins and salidroside are primary bioactives. Some clinical trials on fatigue and stress with moderately positive findings.
remedies.safety: May cause dizziness or dry mouth. Not recommended with bipolar disorder or SSRIs without provider guidance.
remedies.serving: Root tea or decoction.

remedies.name: Black Pepper
remedies.summary: 'King of Spices.' Once used as currency. In Ayurveda, part of Trikatu ('three pungents'). Piperine enhances absorption of many nutrients.
remedies.modern: Piperine enhances curcumin bioavailability by up to 2000%. Also enhances other nutrient absorption.
remedies.safety: Very safe in culinary amounts. May increase medication absorption — consult provider.
remedies.serving: Used freely as culinary spice.

remedies.name: Apple Cider Vinegar
preps: 1–2 tbsp diluted in water before meals
remedies.summary: Hippocrates reportedly used vinegar. Staple of American folk medicine. Raw unfiltered ACV with 'the mother' preferred.
remedies.modern: Some modest effects on blood sugar after meals. Acetic acid primary active. Research limited.
remedies.safety: Always dilute — undiluted damages enamel and irritates esophagus. May interact with diabetes meds and diuretics.
remedies.serving: 1–2 tbsp diluted in water. Rinse mouth after.

remedies.name: Chia Seeds
preps: Chia fresca — seeds in water with lime (traditional)
remedies.summary: 'Chia' from Nahuatl for 'oily.' Aztec warriors and Mayan people consumed as staple energy food. Tarahumara runners use for endurance.
remedies.modern: Rich in ALA omega-3, fiber, minerals. Gel-forming property may support hydration and regularity.
remedies.safety: Consume with liquid (dry seeds can expand). High fiber — introduce gradually.
remedies.serving: 1–2 tbsp daily, soaked or in food.

remedies.name: Hops
remedies.summary: Most famous for brewing beer, but long history in herbalism for calming and sleep. Hop pickers reported drowsiness during harvest. Often paired with valerian.
remedies.modern: 2-methyl-3-buten-2-ol may have sedative properties. Hops + valerian studied with moderately positive results.
remedies.safety: Not for those with depression. May interact with sedatives. Not in pregnancy. Estrogenic compounds — hormone-sensitive: consult provider.
remedies.serving: Tea or with valerian before bedtime.

remedies.name: Eucalyptus
preps: Steam inhalation — leaves in hot water (NOT ingested)
remedies.summary: Used by Aboriginal Australians for thousands of years. Adopted into European practice in 19th century. Primarily aromatic/topical — NOT for internal use.
remedies.modern: 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol) is primary active. Some research on respiratory comfort.
remedies.safety: EXTERNAL/AROMATIC ONLY — oil toxic if ingested. Not for infants/young children. Dilute before topical use.
remedies.serving: Steam inhalation or external aromatic use only. Never ingest.
